metadata.subject: English Language Arts
metadata.description: English Language Arts 10 is the integrated core ELA course for Grade 10, developing students' abilities to read, write, communicate, and think critically across a wide range of text forms and purposes. Students engage with complex literary and informational texts, develop sophisticated writing skills, deepen their critical and creative thinking, and explore how language, identity, and culture intersect. First Peoples perspectives on language, story, and communication are central to the course. ELA 10 prepares students for the specialized ELA courses at Grades 11 and 12.
metadata.general_description: The core Grade 10 ELA course integrating literary study, analytical and creative writing, oral communication, and critical thinking — preparing students for senior ELA specializations.

big_ideas.statement: Exploring stories and other texts helps us understand ourselves and make connections to others and the world.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students engage deeply with literary and non-literary texts from diverse cultures, periods, and perspectives — developing empathy, broadening worldview, and building personal meaning.

big_ideas.statement: Effective communication requires thoughtful consideration of purpose, audience, and form.
big_ideas.elaboration: Grade 10 students develop the ability to make increasingly sophisticated rhetorical choices — selecting forms, registers, and strategies appropriate to their communicative goals.

big_ideas.statement: Language and story are powerful tools for personal and community identity.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students examine how language choices, narrative structures, and literary traditions reflect and shape identity — including cultural, gendered, and Indigenous identities.

big_ideas.statement: Questioning what we hear, read, and view contributes to our sense of identity and helps us understand and question our world.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students develop critical thinking skills to analyze texts for perspective, bias, and rhetorical purpose — and apply these skills across literary, informational, and digital texts.

competencies.raw: Apply appropriate strategies to comprehend written, oral, visual, and multimodal texts; analyze how texts use literary elements, techniques, and devices to create meaning; examine how multiple perspectives are presented in texts; connect prior knowledge and personal experience to make inferences and draw conclusions; recognize and respect First Peoples perspectives, knowledge, and ways of knowing in texts.
i_can_statements: I can apply a range of comprehension strategies to understand complex written, oral, visual, and multimodal texts.
I can analyze how literary elements — character, conflict, theme, point of view, symbolism — create meaning in texts.
I can identify and evaluate how different perspectives are presented, privileged, or silenced in texts.
I can make meaningful inferences and connections between texts and my own experience, other texts, and the world.
I can recognize and respectfully engage with First Peoples storytelling traditions, perspectives, and ways of knowing.

competencies.raw: Create thoughtful, engaging, and technically proficient written, oral, visual, and multimodal texts; apply writing processes including planning, drafting, revising, and editing; use language, structure, and form purposefully for specific audiences and purposes; demonstrate growing command of conventions, grammar, and style.
i_can_statements: I can create polished written texts — analytical essays, creative writing, persuasive writing — demonstrating purpose and craft.
I can apply a full writing process: generating ideas, drafting, revising for content and style, and editing for conventions.
I can make deliberate choices about form, structure, language, and voice to communicate effectively.
I can demonstrate command of grammar, punctuation, and usage conventions in my writing.
I can create effective oral and multimodal communications for a range of purposes and audiences.

areas.name: Literary Texts
areas.raw: Reading and analyzing literary texts from diverse genres, cultures, and time periods.
topics: Novel study: full-length fiction from diverse authors and cultural perspectives
Short fiction: analysis of plot, character, setting, conflict, theme, and literary devices
Poetry: figurative language, imagery, sound devices, form, and poetic voice
Drama: script reading, performance elements, and dramatic structure
First Peoples literature: stories, legends, oral transcriptions, and contemporary Indigenous authors
Comparative literature: analyzing texts across time periods, cultures, and perspectives

areas.name: Non-Fiction and Informational Texts
areas.raw: Reading and analyzing non-fiction, informational, and persuasive texts.
topics: Non-fiction forms: memoir, essay, journalism, documentary, and report
Rhetorical analysis: identifying and evaluating persuasive strategies in non-fiction texts
Media and digital texts: evaluating online sources, articles, and multimedia for credibility
Bias and perspective: identifying whose voices are centered and whose are marginalized
Reading research: using text features, skimming, scanning, and synthesizing multiple sources
Cross-text synthesis: connecting ideas across literary and informational sources

areas.name: Analytical and Expository Writing
areas.raw: Analytical essay writing, argumentation, and expository communication.
topics: Literary essay: thesis development, textual evidence, analysis, and MLA or APA formatting
Argumentative writing: claim, evidence, warrant, counter-argument, and rebuttal
Expository writing: explanation, description, and synthesis of ideas across sources
Research writing: gathering, evaluating, citing, and integrating sources
Paragraph structure: topic sentence, development, elaboration, and transitions
Revision strategies: peer feedback, self-assessment, and improving clarity and depth

areas.name: Creative Writing
areas.raw: Creative writing across genres with attention to craft and voice.
topics: Narrative writing: plot structure, character development, dialogue, and pacing
Descriptive and sensory writing: showing vs. telling and creating vivid, concrete imagery
Poetry writing: experimenting with form, voice, imagery, and sound
Personal narrative and memoir: developing authentic voice and reflective depth
Experimental and hybrid forms: blending genres and modes for creative effect
Writing workshop: sharing, workshopping, and revising creative work with peers

areas.name: Oral and Multimodal Communication
areas.raw: Oral presentation, discussion, and multimodal communication.
topics: Formal presentation: organizing and delivering content-focused oral presentations
Socratic seminar and discussion: engaging in evidence-based academic dialogue
Oral interpretation of literature: reading aloud and performing literary texts
Multimodal texts: creating and analyzing presentations combining text, image, and audio
Collaborative communication: group projects, debates, and peer discussion
Digital communication: blogs, vlogs, podcasts, and digital storytelling
